meta.title: Mercedes A‑Class W177 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.h1: Mercedes A‑Class W177 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fourth‑generation A‑Class hatchback — from the Renault‑developed M282 1.3‑litre petrol to the 421PS AMG M139 and the OM608/OM654 diesel range that splits this generation’s ownership story. Backed by real UK costs, honest failure data, and the cylinder‑head warranty that defines the M260’s reputation. Backed by 520+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes A‑Class W177 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W177 hatchback specifically — including the OM608 diesel (Renault K9K‑based), the M282 Renault‑Nissan‑Mercedes co‑development, and the M139 that powers the most powerful production four‑cylinder in the world.

overview.intro: The W177 is the fourth‑generation A‑Class, launched in April 2018 as the successor to the W176. It introduced the MFA2 platform, a longer wheelbase, and — for the first time — a range of Renault‑Nissan‑Alliance co‑developed engines alongside Mercedes’ own M260 and AMG‑tuned M139 units. A significant 2023 facelift brought mild‑hybrid assistance to petrol models and a revised interior, but the engine line‑up remained largely unchanged.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: MFA2 (Mercedes‑Benz Front Architecture, second generation) • Predecessor: W176 (third‑generation A‑Class, 2012–2018) • Successor: Current generation (production ongoing as of 2026) • Sibling body styles in this generation: V177 (saloon), Z177 (long‑wheelbase saloon)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W177 A‑Class generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM654 diesel and M260 petrol variants as the generation enters its seventh year of production and warranty‑age cars begin to need attention.

items.quote: “The most refined diesel in the W177 range. Taxi‑fleet examples regularly exceed 350,000 km with proper servicing; the 190 PS version delivers genuine performance without the cylinder‑head anxiety of the M260 petrol.” Who it’s for: Buyers who need diesel economy and six‑figure mileage potential.

“The sweet spot of the Renault‑Nissan‑Mercedes co‑development. 163 PS is plenty for the W177’s weight, the cylinder‑deactivation system saves fuel at motorway speeds, and the reliability record is better than the M260’s.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want a petrol W177 without the M260’s cylinder‑head anxiety.

“Cylinder‑head failure is this engine’s defining risk — misfires that persist after plug and coil replacement point to valve‑seat issues. Mercedes extended the warranty on cylinder heads to 15 years in some markets, which tells you everything.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the risk and have full service history.

“The most powerful production four‑cylinder in the world — but also one of the most costly to fix. Oil leaks, carbon buildup, cooling system issues and HPFP failure can add up fast; a full replacement runs well into five figures.” Who it’s for: Performance buyers with deep pockets and warranty cover.

“Renault K9K‑based, proven over millions of miles across Europe, and the cheapest diesel to replace in the W177 range. DPF and EGR maintenance is the only real demand.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want a cheap‑to‑run diesel hatchback without the OM654’s premium.

“The entry‑level 109 PS version is often dismissed as underpowered, but it’s the lightest, cheapest‑to‑run petrol in the range — and shares the same fundamental reliability as the A 200. For city driving, it’s the sensible choice.” Who it’s for: City drivers who prioritise running costs over performance.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for cylinder‑head work
Most Expensive — fast, but costly to maintain
Best Value — proven Renault K9K base
Best Diesel — Mercedes’ most reliable modern diesel

economicsRule.title: The W177 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,200 M282 recon against a £12,000 W177 sits at 27% of the car’s value — safe. A £6,500 M260 recon against a £10,000 W177 sits at 65% — that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a sub‑£8,000 W177, the same M260 recon tips over 80% — we’ll honestly tell you to walk away. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W177 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM608 (A 180 d) — cheap DPF/EGR maintenance, proven K9K base.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W177 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M139 (A 45/A 45 S) — oil leaks, carbon, HPFP and cooling system repairs add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W177 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM654 (A 200 d) — modern diesel refinement without the M260’s cylinder‑head anxiety.

problems.title: M260 Cylinder‑Head Failure (Valve Seats)
problems.rootCause: Exhaust valve seats wear prematurely, causing compression loss and misfires that persist after plug and coil replacement. Mercedes issued an extended warranty on M260 cylinder heads in some markets (15 years / 241,000 km), confirming this as a recognised design weakness.
problems.recommendation: If your W177 A 250 or A 35 AMG develops a persistent misfire that doesn’t clear with new plugs and coils, get the cylinder head inspected immediately — a £1,500–£3,000 head replacement is dramatically cheaper than the £3,800–£6,500 full engine replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M260 engine prices for your W177 A 250 →

problems.title: OM608 DPF / EGR Clogging (A 180 d)
problems.rootCause: The Renault‑derived OM608 diesel is sensitive to short‑trip urban driving. Inadequate oil lubrication and debris from the old turbo can accelerate DPF and EGR failure. The engine requires regular motorway runs to regenerate the DPF.
problems.recommendation: If your A 180 d is used mainly for urban driving, schedule a DPF inspection every 20,000 miles — a £400–£1,200 preventative clean is far cheaper than the £1,500–£2,800 replacement that follows a blocked DPF.

cta.label: Compare OM608 engine prices for your W177 A 180 d →

problems.title: M282 Carbon Buildup (Intake Valves)
problems.rootCause: Direct‑injection petrol engines suffer carbon buildup on intake valves because fuel never washes the valves. The M282 is no exception — cold‑start hesitation and reduced power are the early signs.
problems.recommendation: Budget for intake valve cleaning at 60,000 miles on any M282‑powered W177 — a £300–£600 preventative clean is cheap insurance against the £1,500–£3,200 replacement that follows if carbon causes valve‑seat damage.

cta.label: Compare M282 engine prices for your W177 A 200 →

problems.title: M139 Oil Leaks & Cooling System (A 45 / A 45 S)
problems.affectedModels: W177 A 45 AMG, A 45 S AMG (2020–present).
problems.rootCause: The most powerful production four‑cylinder in the world runs high boost and generates significant heat. Oil leaks from gaskets and seals, carbon buildup on intake valves, cooling system leaks, and HPFP failure are the main concerns.
problems.recommendation: If you own a W177 A 45 or A 45 S, treat oil and coolant system inspections as annual maintenance — £800–£2,500 now is far cheaper than the £4,500–£9,000 replacement that follows a neglected leak or overheating event.

cta.label: Compare M139 engine prices for your W177 A 45 →

replacementCosts.h2: W177 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Engine Page baseline figures — W177 hatchback shares identical engine‑bay access with its V177 saloon sibling, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W177 hatchback only. V177 saloon and Z177 long‑wheelbase saloon share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The A 250 e plug‑in hybrid is covered on its own Variant Page.
